title: Introducing this site
summary: A quick note on what this site is for, and why it was built in a fast, deliberately simple first pass.

content.text: This site is a simple place to collect selected projects, product work, and short notes about how they are being built. Rather than scatter that context across different profiles and repos, it felt better to put everything in one clear home.

The first version came together quickly because the goal was not to design a huge platform. It was to get something useful online: a lightweight homepage, a small projects section, and a blog that can publish static posts without adding a CMS or extra infrastructure.

items: The site uses a straightforward Next.js setup with static generation.
Projects and writing are presented with just enough structure to be easy to update.
The blog content lives in JSON, which keeps publishing fast and the build predictable.

content.text: That quick development pace was part of the point. A personal site should be easy to maintain, easy to extend, and useful from day one. This version does that, and it leaves plenty of room to refine the details over time.

title: Designing a static AI-assisted blog
summary: A simple pattern for publishing weekly posts from JSON while keeping validation strict and deployment predictable.

content.text: This site stays deliberately simple: blog posts live in a JSON file, the build validates them, and GitHub Pages serves the exported result.

items: The content format is explicit, so an AI writer has a clear contract to follow.
The build fails early if a post has the wrong shape or an invalid slug.
Deployment stays static, with no CMS or runtime database to manage.

content.text: That combination keeps the authoring flow lightweight without making the site fragile.

title: Weekly coding update, auth, concept flow, and a new site
summary: A week spent turning Draft Path into more of a real product, while also getting a personal site and static blog live on GitHub Pages.

content.text: This week split neatly across two repositories. Most of the deeper product work happened in Draft Path, while a separate push got this personal site live with a small static blog and GitHub Pages deployment.
Draft Path started to feel like a real app

The biggest change was authentication. Draft Path picked up a substantial NextAuth v5 integration, including credentials sign-in and OAuth options for Google and GitHub. That work touched 45 files in PR #15, added auth routes and forms, tightened sign-in error handling, preserved callback redirects, and included a fair amount of follow-on cleanup around validation, route protection, and session shape.

items: PR #15 merged with credentials plus Google and GitHub auth, protected-route updates, and supporting test coverage.
PR #14 added an AI-powered concept clarification flow at /new, backed by schema and migration changes, UI updates, and test fixes.
Smaller follow-up work tightened the UI: a reusable Button component landed in PR #17, the navbar was simplified in PR #20, and the npm lockfile was refreshed in PR #21.

content.text: What I like about that set of changes is that it was not just feature accumulation. The thread running through it was turning rough edges into something more coherent: clearer auth flows, better form behaviour, more reusable UI primitives, and a concept-entry path that feels much closer to the actual product.
The personal site also went from blank to publishable

Alongside the app work, I stood up the mikebarr repository and pushed it through to a working static site. Over a short run of commits, that meant setting up GitHub Pages deployment, adding a homepage and project showcase, creating blog index and post pages, switching blog content to a validated JSON contract, and then publishing the first posts through that system.

items: Set up the GitHub Pages deployment flow for the site.
Built the homepage, project card, and blog pages in Next.js.
Added the blog schema and example data so posts can be published as static content with build-time validation.

content.text: So the week was a good mix of infrastructure and product detail: getting a personal publishing setup online, while also making Draft Path more usable and more believable as an application people could actually sign into and use.
